content:
# Joshua Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 228 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_004_page_001.jpg`

---

**1** And it was, when all the nation had finished crossing the Jordan, that YHWH spoke to Joshua, saying:

**2** "Take for yourselves from the people twelve men, one man from each tribe.

**3** And command them, saying, 'Take up for yourselves from here, from the midst of the Jordan, from the place where the feet of the priests stood firm, twelve stones, and carry them over with you and set them down at the lodging place where you shall lodge tonight.'"

**4** And Joshua called the twelve men whom he had appointed from the sons of Israel, one man from each tribe.

**5** And Joshua said to them, "Pass over before the ark of YHWH your God into the midst of the Jordan, and each of you lift up one stone upon his shoulder, according to the number of the tribes of the sons of Israel,

**6** so that this may be a sign among you. When your sons ask in time to come, saying, 'What do these stones mean to you?'

**7** then you shall say to them, 'Because the waters of the Jordan were cut off before the ark of the covenant of YHWH — when it crossed the Jordan, the waters of the Jordan were cut off.' And these stones shall be a memorial for the sons of Israel forever."

**8** And the sons of Israel did so, just as Joshua commanded. And they took up twelve stones from the midst of the Jordan, as YHWH had spoken to Joshua, according to the number of the tribes of the sons of Israel. And they carried them over with them to the lodging place and set them down there.

**9** And Joshua set up twelve stones in the midst of the Jordan, at the place where the feet of the priests bearing the ark of the covenant had stood. And they are there to this day.

**10** And the priests bearing the ark stood in the midst of the Jordan until every word was completed that YHWH had commanded Joshua to speak to the people, according to all that Moses had commanded Joshua. And the people hurried and crossed over.

**11** And it was, when all the people had finished crossing, that the ark of YHWH crossed over, and the priests, before the people.

**12** And the sons of Reuben and the sons of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh crossed over armed before the sons of Israel, as Moses had spoken to them.

**13** About forty thousand equipped for war crossed over before YHWH for battle, to the plains of Jericho.

**14** On that day YHWH made Joshua great in the eyes of all Israel, and they feared him as they had feared Moses all the days of his life.

**15** And YHWH spoke to Joshua, saying:

**16** "Command the priests bearing the ark of the testimony to come up from the Jordan."

**17** And Joshua commanded the priests, saying, "Come up from the Jordan."

**18** And it was, when the priests bearing the ark of the covenant of YHWH came up from the midst of the Jordan, and the soles of the priests' feet were drawn up onto dry ground, that the waters of the Jordan returned to their place and flowed over all its banks as before.

**19** And the people came up from the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month, and they encamped at Gilgal, on the eastern border of Jericho.

**20** And those twelve stones which they had taken from the Jordan, Joshua set up at Gilgal.

**21** And he spoke to the sons of Israel, saying, "When your sons ask their fathers in time to come, saying, 'What are these stones?'

**22** then you shall make known to your sons, saying, 'On dry ground Israel crossed over this Jordan,

**23** for YHWH your God dried up the waters of the Jordan before you until you crossed over, just as YHWH your God did to the Sea of Reeds, which He dried up before us until we crossed over —

**24** so that all the peoples of the earth may know the hand of YHWH, that it is mighty, so that you may fear YHWH your God all the days.'"

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscript
This translation was produced from direct reading of the Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A), dated c. 1008 CE, page 228. The photographs are from the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). The text of Joshua 4 appears in the codex's characteristic three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation. The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, 4th century CE) was consulted as a secondary witness via the Vatican Digital Library.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **גּוֹי (goy) — "nation" (v. 1):** The text uses גּוֹי rather than עַם (am, "people"). These are distinct terms in Biblical Hebrew: goy emphasizes a nation as a political-ethnic body, while am tends toward kinship and communal relationship. The distinction is preserved. In verse 10, where the text shifts to עָם (am), the translation correspondingly renders "people." This alternation is deliberate in the Hebrew and is maintained without harmonization.

2. **אִישׁ אֶחָד אִישׁ אֶחָד (ish echad ish echad) — "one man from each tribe" (vv. 2, 4):** The Hebrew uses a distributive repetition — literally "one man, one man from a tribe." A strictly literal rendering would preserve the doubled phrase, but the LOGOS translation smooths this to "one man from each tribe" as a readable formal equivalence. The distributive sense is preserved; the mechanical repetition is not. This is a point where LOGOS diverges from a maximally literal rendering in the interest of English transparency — the doubled idiom has no corresponding English construction and would read as accidental repetition rather than deliberate distribution.

3. **הָכִין (hakhin) — "stood firm" (v. 3):** The Hiphil infinitive absolute of כּוּן (kun, "to be firm, established") describes the posture of the priests' feet in the riverbed. Rendered "stood firm" rather than the more mechanical "firmly" to give the English reader a clear image of stable, established positioning — feet planted and unmoving in the midst of a dried riverbed.

4. **הֵכִין (hekhin) — "appointed" (v. 4):** The same root כּוּן in the Hiphil perfect, referring to Joshua's prior selection of the twelve men (cf. 3:12). Rendered "appointed" rather than "prepared" to reflect the deliberate act of designation. The verbal echo between vv. 3 and 4 — the same root describing both the priests' firm stance and Joshua's firm arrangement — is a wordplay that English cannot fully preserve, but the connection is noted here.

5. **Two sets of twelve stones (vv. 8–9):** The Masoretic Text describes two distinct stone installations: twelve stones taken from the Jordan and carried to the camp (vv. 3, 8, 20), and twelve stones that Joshua set up within the Jordan itself at the place where the priests stood (v. 9). Some interpreters have attempted to harmonize these as a single set, but the Hebrew text presents them as separate acts, and the formal equivalence translation preserves both without editorial resolution. The note "and they are there to this day" (v. 9) applies specifically to the stones in the riverbed — a remarkable claim, since they would be submerged once the waters returned.

6. **אֲרוֹן הָעֵדֻת (aron ha'edut) — "ark of the testimony" (v. 16) vs. אֲרוֹן בְּרִית יהוה (aron berit YHWH) — "ark of the covenant of YHWH" (vv. 7, 9, 18):** The Masoretic Text alternates between these designations within a single chapter. This is not carelessness but reflects two theological traditions regarding the ark's identity: as the container of the covenant (berit) and as the container of the testimony (edut). The LOGOS translation preserves the alternation exactly as it appears in the Hebrew, without harmonizing to a single title. The LXX (Vaticanus) consistently uses κιβωτὸς τῆς διαθήκης κυρίου ("ark of the covenant of the Lord"), smoothing the MT's variation — a characteristic LXX tendency toward consistency that the formal equivalence translation declines to follow.

7. **נִתְּקוּ (nitequ) — "were drawn up" (v. 18):** From the root נתק (nataq), meaning "to pull away, to tear loose, to be drawn out." This verb is more forceful than a simple "lifted" — it conveys the dramatic severance of the priests' feet from the riverbed, the decisive moment when divine intervention ceased and the natural order resumed. The Niphal conjugation indicates the passive: the feet were drawn up, as if pulled by an external force, not merely stepped away.

8. **Person shift in vv. 23–24:** The speech shifts from second person ("before you... until you crossed over") to first person ("before us until we crossed over"). This collapse of temporal distance is theologically deliberate: the speaker identifies the present generation with the generation that crossed. Future Israelite fathers are instructed to say "until we crossed over" — not "until they crossed over." The memorial stones do not merely preserve history; they make every generation a participant in the event. The shift is preserved without correction.

9. **יַם־סוּף (yam suf) — "Sea of Reeds" (v. 23):** Rendered "Sea of Reeds" rather than "Red Sea." The Hebrew סוּף means "reed, rush" (cf. Exod 2:3, 5, of the reeds among which Moses' basket was placed). The identification with the modern Red Sea derives from the LXX's ἐρυθρὰ θάλασσα (erythra thalassa, "Red Sea"), which is a geographic identification, not a translation of the Hebrew. A formal equivalence translation renders what the Hebrew says: Sea of Reeds.

10. **כַּאֲשֶׁר (ka'asher) — "just as" (vv. 8, 23):** This comparative particle links the Jordan crossing explicitly to the exodus event (v. 23) and Joshua's actions to YHWH's command (v. 8). Rendered "just as" to preserve the exactness of the comparison — the Jordan miracle is presented as a precise typological parallel to the Sea of Reeds miracle, not merely similar but formally equivalent.

11. **Gilgal (v. 19):** The name גִּלְגָּל (Gilgal) is related to the root גלל (galal, "to roll"), a connection made explicit in 5:9 where YHWH says "I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you." The place name is transliterated rather than translated, as it functions as a proper noun in this context. The etymological wordplay becomes operative in the following chapter.

12. **לְמַעַן (lema'an) — purpose clauses (v. 24):** The verse contains two purpose clauses: "so that all the peoples of the earth may know" and "so that you may fear." The first is universal (all peoples), the second is particular (Israel). The memorial stones serve a dual theological purpose: they are a witness to the nations of YHWH's power, and they are a perpetual summons to Israel's own reverence. Both purpose clauses are rendered with "so that" to preserve the parallel construction.

### LXX Observations (Vaticanus)

The LXX at Joshua 4 follows the MT closely in structure and content. Several differences merit note:

- **Harmonization of ark titles:** As noted above, the LXX consistently renders "ark of the covenant of the Lord" where the MT alternates between "covenant" and "testimony." The LOGOS translation follows the MT's variation.
- **v. 12 — Tribal details:** The LXX expands slightly on the Transjordanian tribes' crossing, but the substance is identical. The MT's concise formulation is retained.
- **v. 18 — nitequ:** The LXX renders the priests' emergence from the Jordan somewhat differently, using ἐξέβησαν ("they went out") rather than preserving the forceful "drawn up" sense of the Hebrew נתק. The LOGOS translation follows the MT's more vivid verb.
- **v. 24 — Universal scope:** Both the MT and LXX preserve the universalizing purpose clause ("all the peoples of the earth"). This is not a late theological addition but is present in both the oldest Hebrew and Greek witnesses.

— LOGOS

---

*The LOGOS Bible — The Word, rendered faithfully.*
*Joshua 4 translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 228.*
